data.name: Nawaf Al-Aqidi
data.description: Saudi Arabia squeezed through to the World Cup thanks in part to a fine injury-time save from al-Aqidi in the final playoff against Iraq. Saudi Arabia needed a draw and the game finished 0-0. “We had to hold our breath but he came through for us as we knew he would,” said the previous coach Hervé Renard. The goalkeeper added: "We have achieved something extraordinary, truly unbelievable.” Al-Aqidi was in the squad for the 2022 tournament but very much the third-choice and never saw a single minute but in the same year was the star of the under-23s team who won the West Asian Football Federation Championship title without conceding a single goal. Since then, the Riyadh-born al-Aqidi star has, like most Saudi goalkeepers, been looking for regular playing time at home amid an influx of foreign rivals. Went on loan to Al-Fateh for the second part of the 2024-25 season and did well but returned to Al-Nassr to be in and out of the side.

data.name: Ali Majrashi
data.description: Having come through the Saudi scholarship programme for developing football talents as a teenager, Ali Majarshi has matured impressively as Al-Ahli’s resurgence has gathered pace. Calm in possession and energetic in his forward bursts, he has become a reliable outlet down the right flank, contributing a steady stream of assists and overlapping runs that stretch opposition defences. His versatility allows him to operate further up the pitch when needed, adding flexibility to the Jeddah club’s attacking patterns. A product of Saudi football’s new generation, Majarshi blends technical quality with aggression – though his competitive streak occasionally sees him tread a fine disciplinary line.

data.name: Ali Lajami
data.description: Lajami upset Al-Nassr by leaving on a free transfer to join their bitter rivals Al-Hilal in 2025. It was quite the controversy but the move was seen as the right one for the intelligent centre-back, who is able to bring the ball out of defence. “Lajami has great potential and I think Al-Hilal see in him the qualities they want – he needs the right environment,” said the former international midfielder Mohammed Noor. It looked as if his World Cup was over and he was dropped from the March squad by Hervé Renard but has been given a reprieve by the new coach, Georgios Donis. His twin brother, Qassem, has three caps for Saudi Arabia and was most recently on loan at Al-Taawoun.

data.name: Abdulelah Al-Amri
data.description: A leader on and off the pitch, perhaps it is the fact that the centre-back hails from the city of Taif in the Hijaz mountains in the west of the country, that he seems to have an air of calmness and stability. Any games at altitude will also not be a problem. Played all three games at the 2022 World Cup but has been in and out of the national team since, though Hervé Renard was more of a fan than his predecessor, Roberto Mancini, was. Now it remains to be seen what Georgios Donis makes of him. He may be strong in the air and tidy on the ball but he is no longer an automatic choice for club or country, though gives everything when called upon. Back at Al-Nassr for the 2025-26 season having been on loan at Al-Ittihad the previous campaign.

data.name: Hassan Al-Tambakti
data.description: “Tambakti is the best Saudi player at the moment, without a doubt,” said the former international Abdullah Otayf on TV in November. “He should be playing in Europe as he has all the tools.” There has been interest from clubs in France and Portugal but the 27-year-old has stayed in Riyadh. A leader on the pitch and more than holds his own against the top-class strikers that have been imported into the Saudi Pro League. Powerful and confident with the ball, he has been compared to the former Inter defender Milan Skriniar, and has the physical strength to mix it with any forward.

data.name: Nasser Al-Dawsari
data.description: According to local reports, several European clubs – such as Cercle Brugge and Sporting Braga – inquired about the technical 27-year-old last summer but got short shrift from the four-time Asian champions after his impressive performances at the Club World Cup, where he outshone some bigger names on the global stage. He does like a big game as he showed with a spectacular strike in the final of the 2021 Asian Champions League. Plays most of the games for Al-Hilal as his coach, Simone Inzaghi, is a fan of his versatility and intelligence. He is in fine form and looks ready to step out of the shadow of his more famous namesake in the team.

data.name: Musab Al-Juwayr
data.description: “He makes the game look easy and has a very bright future, performing better when the game is bigger and that is a special gift,” said Vítor Pereira, Al-Juwayr’s former coach who has reportedly been interested in taking the playmaker to England. In a league where young players do not always get opportunities, Al-Juwayr has been a shining light. A deserved winner of the 2024-25 young player of the season award, he won the monthly gong no fewer than eight times. Blessed with composure and vision, he has been assisting goals like it is going out of fashion and played a leading role in World Cup qualification.

data.name: Ayman Yahya
data.description: Was the player of the tournament and top scorer as Saudi Arabia won the Under-23 Asian Cup in 2022 and the winger, who came through Al-Nassr’s youth academy, was tipped as the next big thing. Hervé Renard gave Yahya his international debut as a teenager in 2022, rare in Saudi Arabia, but he has not quite kicked on. Perhaps his versatility – he can play on either side in defence or attack as well as an advanced central role – has counted against the Riyadh-born player. A fast player who likes to get forward, he has suffered a couple of injuries this season for Al-Nassr.

data.name: Firas Al-Buraikan
data.description: The perfect example of what many, many Saudi strikers are going through at the moment. Started out at Al-Nassr, before moving to Al-Fateh for more minutes. There, he found playing time and the net on a regular basis, showing composure in front of goal and an ability to find space in crowded areas. Impressively, he finished as the league’s fourth top scorer in 2023 and fifth the year after. Then there was a move back to a big club, Al-Ahli, where he started well, but after the Jeddah club signed Ivan Toney al-Buraikan was shifted to the wing.

data.name: Salem Al-Dawsari
data.description: His winner followed by a somersault against Argentina was one of the moments of the 2022 World Cup and he has continued to be one of the standouts in the star-studded Saudi Pro League thanks to his creativity and vision. There are concerns, however, that his national team form is not what it was and a number of missed penalties in qualification almost cost Saudi Arabia a place at the World Cup. When the going gets tough, however, the 2025 Asian player of the year is still the one the others look to.

data.name: Saleh Al-Shehri
data.description: An old-fashioned No 9 who likes to let defenders know he is around, get stuck in, chase lost causes and generally be a pain. Scored the equaliser against Argentina in the 2-1 win at the 2022 World Cup but he has still suffered from the same problem that all Saudi strikers have at the moment in that he is now a bit-part player for Al-Ittihad and he is likely to be the same for the national team. "The presence of a large number of foreigners in the league has two sides,” he said recently. “The first is positive, with the opportunity to interact with the best international players, such as playing alongside Karim Benzema. The negative side is somewhat limited [opportunities], with fewer playing minutes.”

data.name: Saud Abdulhamid
data.description: The only senior Saudi player with substantial European experience in one of the big-five European leagues. While the goalscorers got the headlines after the 2-1 World Cup win over Argentina, most at home agreed that the attack-minded right-back was the standout for Saudi Arabia at that tournament. There was pride when Juventus were interested and excitement when he signed for  Roma. After struggling for game time in Italy, however, he was loaned out to Ligue 1 title-chasing Lens. Is not a regular starter in northern France but has filled in without much fuss when called upon, leading to links with Everton and Newcastle. In March he became the first Saudi player to score in France’s top flight. Expectations will be high in the summer.

data.name: Nawaf Boushal
data.description: Hails from the Unesco heritage site of Ahsa, which is famous for producing dates. The speedy right-back, who can play on either side, has carved out a name for himself as well as a furrow up and down that side of the pitch. “His is a true story of perseverance,” said the TV pundit Ali al-Marshud. “A player who was once close to leaving [Al-Nassr] because it was said his services were no longer needed, but he made great sacrifices and became a top performer, earning the trust of coaches at both club and the national team.” As well as defensive discipline, he has become an increasing threat going forward, making 11 goal contributions for Al-Nassr in 2024-25.

data.name: Hassan Kadesh
data.description: Was first selected by Bert van Marwijk back in 2016 but Roberto Mancini was the first national-team coach to really give this genial centre-back a run in the team. Born in the eastern city of Dammam, Kadesh became the most expensive Saudi player in history in 2023 with a £12m transfer to Al-Ittihad, a move made even more impressive by the fact that he was just a month short of his 30th birthday and a defender to boot. There were more headlines when he became the joint top scorer for Saudi Arabia in the main stage of World Cup qualification, scoring both goals, one in the last-minute, in a crucial win over China. His heading ability has been useful in both areas.

data.name: Abdullah Al-Khaibari
data.description: A no-nonsense and intelligent defensive midfielder, Al-Khaibari rarely makes headlines but then rarely makes mistakes. “Every successful team needs a player like him,” said the Al-Nassr coach, Jorge Jesus. “He reads the game well and uses the ball well.” The early signs are that the new national-team coach, Georgios Donis, is a fan and the Riyadh-born midfielder is in North America after a successful season in which he played a big role in Al-Nassr winning the Saudi Pro League title for the first time since 2019. He has noticeably improved since playing and training alongside stars such as Cristiano Ronaldo, Marcelo Brozovic, Sadio Mané and João Félix.

data.name: Ziyad Al-Johani
data.description: Will be one of the fittest players at the World Cup, never stops running and is a more than competent link between defence and midfield. Came up through the youth ranks at Al-Ahli and has stayed there ever since, helping the club to the Asian Champions League in 2025. There was interest from Al-Nassr in 2025 with their coach, Luís Castro, a fan of the midfielder’s vision and leadership qualities on the pitch, but Al-Ahli decided that his partnership with Franck Kessié was too valuable to break up.

data.name: Khalid Al-Ghannam
data.description: Such was Al-Ghannam’s form this season that the left-winger could not be ignored and he was recalled in March after a gap of more than two years. He has simply been exceptional for Al-Ettifaq and was one of the best players in the Saudi Pro League this season. He has always been fast but has more control these days, with 13 goals and seven assists, and loves to beat players with pace and trickery. He could be a great option late in the game in hot conditions and if he shines in North America, the clamour for his signature at home is just going to increase, with Al-Ettifaq's local rivals Al-Qadsiah leading the line of suitors.

data.name: Alaa Al-Hejji
data.description: It’s fair to say the 30-year-old would not have made his international debut in May under Hervé Renard. Georgios Donis, the new head coach, had worked with the midfielder over two spells at Al-Wehda and quickly came to trust the player who started his journey in futsal. In the beginning, Al-Hejji was a defensive midfielder but, under Donis, he was also used further up the pitch. After spending his career at smaller clubs, finally got a big move to the ambitious Neom last summer. It took a little while to get playing time in the early part of the season but soon was in the starting XI to help the promoted club finish in mid-table.

data.name: Abdullah Al-Hamdan
data.description: The son of the former Al-Shabab goalkeeper Abdulrahman al-Hamdan, the striker broke through domestically with his goalscoring instincts and was at one point seen as the great hope of Saudi Arabian strikers. In 2021 however, the mighty Al-Hilal swooped to sign the youngster in what was seen as a huge move for Saudi football, the biggest club paying big for local potential. It did not work out, however, with restricted minutes and a handful of league goals over five seasons. Moved across Riyadh in February to join Cristiano Ronaldo but is still struggling for minutes. He offers an aerial presence, hard work and the ability to play across the frontline.

data.name: Sultan Mandash
data.description: A late bolter for the squad and, possibly, the starting XI. That may sound strange for a 31-year-old who from his international debut in 2019 until 2025 had made just three appearances. The right-winger likes to play high up the pitch and use his speed to make things happen and caught the eye after three goals in the last four games of the season for Al-Hilal, especially given Georgios Donis is in charge of a team with attacking problems. Mandash came off the bench in May’s friendly against Ecuador and scored in a 2-1 defeat. The 31-year-old is sure to play some part this summer, either starting or off the bench when goals are needed.

data.name: Mohammed Al-Owais
data.description: Was named man of the match in the famous 2-1 win over Argentina at the 2022 World Cup, a feat made even more impressive given that the Saudi Arabia No 1 was second-choice for his club at the time and that he had to regain his focus after a collision which left his teammate Yasser Al-Shahrani unconscious and unable to carry on. In 2025 Al-Owais tired of playing second fiddle to Morocco's Yassine "Bono" Bounou at Al-Hilal and dropped down a division to help Al-Ula’s promotion push, where he has enjoyed a new lease of life. A calm presence around the team, which means he may well have an important role to play at the World Cup despite the fact he is not expected to be the first-choice this time around.

data.name: Ahmed Al-Kassar
data.description: The oldest player in the squad and, indeed, the oldest Saudi Arabian ever to go to a World Cup. Has been a bit of a journeyman in the domestic league, never playing for one of the Big Four. Despite his advancing years, he only made his international debut in 2024 during the earlier stages of qualification under Roberto Mancini but played little under Hervé Renard. He hasn’t played much this season for Al-Qadsiah either, with Koen Casteels getting the nod from Brendan Rodgers but he came in and did a job when needed and that will be the case at the World Cup too.

data.name: Mohamed Kanno
data.description: This leggy and dynamic box-to-box midfielder has held his own at the all-conquering Al-Hilal since 2017 and is still going strong. “With a bit more maturity, he could be a top-class player,” said the Al-Hilal coach in 2022, Ramón Díaz. Still loses his head from time to time but a succession of national-team coaches, especially Hervé Renard, have selected the player born in Khobar, on the east coast, just across the bay from Bahrain as they appreciate his energy and work rate. Also possesses a fierce shot.

data.name: Moteb Al-Harbi
data.description: “Al-Harbi has everything needed to become a top-class left-back,” Hervé Renard said when selecting him for an international debut in 2021. Many agreed. After impressing for Al-Shabab, playing over 100 games, scoring five and getting forward to record 12 assists, he became the most expensive Saudi Arabian player in 2024 when Al-Hilal reportedly paid over £20m for his services – though there was controversy when Al-Nassr claimed the player had actually agreed to join them. Has had a solid season for the Blues but has yet to make his mark internationally. That could be about to change.

data.name: Jehad Thakri
data.description: It didn’t take long for Brendan Rodgers, after he arrived to take over Al-Qadsiah, to offer Thakri a new contract. The 25-year-old has earned plenty of praise from Fernando Nacho, his central defensive partner, in the past season and he has become more confident on the ball and more active out of possession. Only made his international debut last year but quickly earned the trust of the then Saudi Arabia coach, Hervé Renard, and played in the crucial game against Iraq that clinched qualification, barely putting a foot wrong and proving he has the mindset for the big occasion.

data.name: Mohammed Abu Al-Shamat
data.description: Wahid Abu al-Shamat must be a proud father. Not quite able to make the footballing grade, he ended up working in the public sector but still found time to ensure that his twin sons had the opportunity to give it their best shot. For a while, he trained them himself, before enrolling them in a Jeddah academy. That was just the start. Having impressed at Al-Qadsiah, Mohammed al-Shamat was recently the subject of a number of bids from Simone Inzaghi’s Al-Hilal, going up to as much as £10m. However, Brendan Rodgers and Al-Qadsiah were not interested and rightly so, as Abu al-Shamat has become one of the best full-backs in the country.
